item.title: Communities in Simbu experiencing full impact of drought
item.description: Simbu Province is staring down a humanitarian crisis as El Niño enters its second month, with drinking water sources drying up, food gardens failing, and bushfires destroying crops and forest. Communities from Konoma to Dinga 1 and Dinga 2 in Suwai LLG of Sinesine-Yongomugl District are feeling the full impact of the drought.
item.content:encoded:
Simbu Province is staring down a humanitarian crisis as El Niño enters its second month, with drinking water sources drying up, food gardens failing, and bushfires destroying crops and forest.

Communities from Konoma to Dinga 1 and Dinga 2 in Suwai LLG of Sinesine-Yongomugl District are feeling the full impact of the drought.

NBC visited the area yesterday and confirmed that Simbu is now heading into its second month of drought.

While there has been some light rain, it has not been enough to revive the land. Instead, crops and drinking water sources have continued to dry up.

In Dinga, villagers are rationing every drop of water, and at Giu Primary School, classes are forced to end at 1pm because all water tanks have run dry.

Joe Gilmai, the Head Teacher of Giu Primary School, said the lack of water is affecting both students and staff, and is making it difficult to maintain basic hygiene at the school.

"The few remaining drinking water sources have reduced drastically. Communities have now issued public notices for people to use water only for drinking and not for other purposes," he said.

Staple foods such as kaukau and greens have also been badly affected by the dry conditions.

Mark Kerenga from Ward 11, Suwai LLG, said if the dry season continues for another month, people will be greatly affected.

"Our gardens are dying. If this continues, we will have nothing to eat," Kerenga said.

John Poiye, a young leader from Dinga 1, said the main freshwater source that serves 5 ward areas has also dried up.

He said they blocked a water source to create a well that supplied 5 ward areas, and that well has now dried up.

George Waikai, Simbu Provincial Disaster Officer, said his office is aware of the situation in the province and is working to address the impacts of the severe El Niño.

Villagers say they are preparing for the worst, but are calling on the government to assist with awareness and training on how to survive such conditions, or to implement other immediate measures.

item.title: Second body recovered in search for missing passengers
item.description: Another body has been recovered in the search for the 27 passengers who went missing three days ago after a boat incident in New Ireland Province. The latest recovery was made early this morning along the coastline of Djaul Island, bringing the total number of bodies recovered to two. Both victims are female, while 25 passengers remain missing.
item.content:encoded:
Another body has been recovered in the search for the 27 passengers who went missing three days ago after a boat incident in New Ireland Province.

The latest recovery was made early this morning along the coastline of Djaul Island, bringing the total number of bodies recovered to two. Both victims are female, while 25 passengers remain missing.

The second body was found about 10 hours after the first victim was recovered from the same coastline.

Authorities say the latest discovery will help guide today's aerial search operations as rescue teams continue searching the surrounding waters.

The first victim has been identified as Melat Victor Touran from Malakuna 4 in Kokopo District. Her body was airlifted to Rabaul Provincial Hospital yesterday afternoon.

The identity of the second victim has not yet been confirmed. Her body was transported by boat to the Rabaul Provincial Hospital morgue at around 10:00 this morning.

Search and rescue efforts are continuing.

item.title: SILAG draft bill sets path for university transition
item.description: The Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance [SILAG] has outlined its proposed governance and financial reforms in a draft legislation as it moves closer to becoming a university. The proposals were presented during a stakeholder consultation workshop held in Port Moresby last week, where government agencies and key stakeholders reviewed the draft bill.
item.content:encoded:
The Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance [SILAG] has outlined its proposed governance and financial reforms in a draft legislation as it moves closer to becoming a university.

The proposals were presented during a stakeholder consultation workshop held in Port Moresby last week, where government agencies and key stakeholders reviewed the draft bill.

SILAG was established to train Papua New Guinea's public servants in leadership, governance and management. As a university, the institute plans to expand its degree programs, research and professional training while continuing its focus on public sector development.

Chief Executive Officer Michael Barobe said the draft legislation proposes replacing the Deputy President position with four Vice Presidents, each responsible for a key area of the university.

"We have proposed four Vice Presidents so each can focus on their responsibilities and strengthen the leadership of the university," Barobe said.

The draft bill also proposes that the President serve as the Chief Executive Officer and Chair of the Academic Board to provide both academic and administrative leadership.

It further proposes that the Chief Secretary to Government become the Chairperson of the University Council in an ex-officio role.

"This will strengthen the link between the university and the National Public Service and ensure our programs continue to support government priorities," he said.

The draft legislation also introduces new bylaws and internal rules to improve governance and decision-making. It also allows the university to enter commercial partnerships and business ventures to generate its own income and reduce reliance on government funding.

"The goal is to build a strong and financially sustainable university while remaining accountable under the Public Finances (Management) Act," Barobe said.

item.title: Four years as acting CEO earns Yavase permanent SEZA appointment
item.description: After serving as Acting Chief Executive Officer of the Special Economic Zones Authority [SEZA] for four years, Kikila Yavase has been appointed as the authority's Chief Executive Officer for the next four years. The appointment was approved by the National Executive Council and announced by International Trade and Investment Minister Richard Maru.
item.content:encoded:
After serving as Acting Chief Executive Officer of the Special Economic Zones Authority [SEZA] for four years, Kikila Yavase has been appointed as the authority's Chief Executive Officer for the next four years.

The appointment was approved by the National Executive Council and announced by International Trade and Investment Minister Richard Maru.

Minister Maru congratulated Mr. Yavase, saying he had earned the position through his commitment and leadership.

"As the foundation Acting CEO, Mr. Yavase has been developed and mentored on the job and has proved himself to be capable with four SEZs delivered under his leadership.

"We have confidence and trust that he will provide the leadership that is required to drive the SEZ agenda of PNG," Minister Maru said.

He said Special Economic Zones are still a new concept in Papua New Guinea and strong leadership is needed to grow the program.

Minister Maru also announced that three more Special Economic Zones are being processed by the SEZA Board and are expected to receive licences within the next three months, subject to meeting all requirements.

He said he expects another three zones. They inckude Sepik Plains SEZ, Madang Integrated SEZ and the Vanimo Free Trade Zone. These SEZs are expected to be approved before the end of the year.

Meanwhile, Minister Maru said Mr. Yavase's immediate priorities include replacing the current SEZ Act with a new law, setting up a Building Board for SEZs across the country, and working with the Department of International Trade and Investment to host the first PNG-China Business Forum and Trade Expo in Guangzhou.

item.title: MVIL begins nationwide health partnership drive to improve insurance claims
item.description: Motor Vehicles Insurance Limited [MVIL] has announced plans to sign similar agreements with Provincial Health Authorities across Papua New Guinea after recently signed its first Memorandum of Understanding [MoU] with the National Capital District Provincial Health Authority [NCDPHA]. The agreement aims to improve the verification of medical reports, speed up insurance claims, and strengthen cooper
item.content:encoded:
Motor Vehicles Insurance Limited [MVIL] has announced plans to sign similar agreements with Provincial Health Authorities across Papua New Guinea after recently signed its first Memorandum of Understanding [MoU] with the National Capital District Provincial Health Authority [NCDPHA].

The agreement aims to improve the verification of medical reports, speed up insurance claims, and strengthen cooperation between the two organisations in supporting road accident victims.

MVIL Chief Executive Officer Bafino Koi said the partnership marks the beginning of a nationwide effort to improve the claims process through closer collaboration with public health authorities.

"This is the first of many partnerships we plan to establish with Provincial Health Authorities across the country," Mr Koi said.

He said verified medical reports prepared by qualified health professionals will help MVIL process claims more quickly while preventing false or excessive payments.

"Most road accident victims receive treatment at public hospitals. Working closely with Provincial Health Authorities is important to ensure claims are processed fairly, accurately and on time," he said.

Under the agreement, MVIL will also support NCDPHA through training and capacity building to strengthen the authority's ability to provide timely and accurate medical reports.

NCDPHA Chief Executive Officer Dr Robin Oge described the agreement as a major milestone for both organizations.

"This partnership will help us better organise our medical and administrative teams to ensure verified medical reports are prepared and submitted efficiently to support the claims process," Dr Oge said.

He said the agreement would improve coordination between the health authority and MVIL, resulting in faster services for people injured in road accidents.

Both organisations said the partnership demonstrates the importance of inter-agency collaboration in delivering more efficient, transparent and people-focused services.

They also expressed confidence that the agreement will serve as a model for similar partnerships with Provincial Health Authorities throughout Papua New Guinea.

item.title: UPNG plans international university centers on campus
item.description: The University of Papua New Guinea [UPNG] is planning to establish partnerships with leading universities from China, the United States and Australia to set up satellite and sister campuses on its Waigani campus. The proposal forms part of UPNG's 20 to 50-year master plan aimed at expanding access to internationally recognised higher education for Papua New Guinean students.
item.content:encoded:
The University of Papua New Guinea [UPNG] is planning to establish partnerships with leading universities from China, the United States and Australia to set up satellite and sister campuses on its Waigani campus.

The proposal forms part of UPNG's 20 to 50-year master plan aimed at expanding access to internationally recognised higher education for Papua New Guinean students.

Vice-Chancellor Professor Ian Findlay said the university wants to create opportunities for students to study programmes offered by overseas universities without having to leave the country.

"We're hoping to build up those collaborations to create university centres that will allow our Papua New Guinean students to go to China, go to America and go to Australia to further their education," Professor Findlay said.

He said the partnerships could also see international universities establish a physical presence at UPNG through sister, mini or satellite campuses.

"These partnerships can result in online campuses. Those campuses can be Chinese universities, American universities and Australian universities having a presence actually at UPNG," he said.

"They can have sister campuses or mini campuses, satellite campuses, at PNG to allow PNG students to study."

Professor Findlay said the university is currently developing a long-term master plan that will guide its growth over the next two to five decades.

"We are currently undergoing a master plan of what we want UPNG to look like in the next 20 to 50 years. That will be part of our master plan, to develop those partnerships with other universities and have the possibility of having satellite campuses either abroad or in PNG," he said.

He said the initiative is intended to strengthen UPNG's international partnerships and make world-class education more accessible to Papua New Guinean students while supporting the university's long-term development.

item.title: PPC Baim urges people not to politicize police recruitment drive
item.description: Intending candidates for the coming 2027 National General Election, supporters as well as the general public in Madang Province have been urged not to politicise the current police recruitment drive that is penetrating right down to the district level. Provincial Police Commander for Madang, Superintendent Robert Baim made the call following rumors in the province that the exercise is tied to cert
item.content:encoded:
Intending candidates for the coming 2027 National General Election, supporters as well as the general public in Madang Province have been urged not to politicise the current police recruitment drive that is penetrating right down to the district level.

Provincial Police Commander for Madang, Superintendent Robert Baim made the call following rumors in the province that the exercise is tied to certain political leaders or is politically motivated.

Mr Baim said that as the nation approaches the 2027 NGE, the Royal PNG Constabulary is extending the recruitment drive into rural districts.

He clarified that the go-rural recruitment drive is an initiative of the Police Commissioner and is an institutional policy to improve police presence in the rural areas of the country.

“It is not a not a political exercise, and definitely not something that should be used for political point- scoring by any individual, political parties or intending candidates.

“This initiative has one purpose, which is to give a fair and equal opportunity to young men and women from the rural parts of the country, who too often miss out on such national opportunities just because of their geographical locations.

“The RPNGC is now giving them the chance to apply for raining and serve the country through the Constabulary,” Superintendent Baim clarified.

Baim added that for far too long recruitment drives have been concentrated around urban centres, leaving capable, willing and disciplined young people in remote and rural districts without a genuine pathway to a policing career. The Commissioner’s plan now changes that.

PPC Baim added, “By taking recruitment teams directly to rural areas, we are removing the barriers of distant, cost, and access that have historically disadvantaged rural applicants, not favouring one region, group, or electorate over another.

“I urge our people in Madang Province to see this initiative as a fair and inclusive national policy; aimed at strengthening the Constabulary with capable recruits from every corner of the country, both urban and rural. The products of this recruitment drive will serve all Papua New Guineans.”

Superintendent Baim stressed that any attempts to claim credit for this initiative, or to use it for political point- scoring ahead of the election, does a disservice to the young people.

“The RPNGC remains a professional, non- partisan institution and recruitment decisions will continue to be made on merit, fitness and suitability, nothing else,” PPC Baim said.

In Madang Province, the recruitment teams are currently in Bogia, Sumkar, Raikos and Usino/ Bundi doing the recruitment awareness and assisting applicants.

item.title: Police freeze K4 Million in suspected stolen DDA funds
item.description: Police have frozen K4 million in suspected stolen public funds held in the bank account of a foreign-owned company. This pushes the total value of assets and funds restrained under anti-money laundering investigations to more than K200 million.
item.content:encoded:
Police have frozen K4 million in suspected stolen public funds held in the bank account of a foreign-owned company.

This pushes the total value of assets and funds restrained under anti-money laundering investigations to more than K200 million.

Police Commissioner David Manning said the money was allegedly paid by a District Development Authority [DDA] to a company owned by a foreign national for the supply of infrastructure components.

Commissioner Manning said a total of K7 million had been paid to the company, with K3 million withdrawn in cash, while the remaining K4 million has now been frozen.

He said the case is one of several active investigations aimed at recovering stolen public funds and prosecuting those responsible.

"The message is simple, if you steal public money, we will find it, freeze it and take it back. If you helped move it, hide it or ignored the warning signs, you are also on notice," Commissioner Manning said.

He said the Proceeds of Crime Act 2022 has strengthened the powers of police and partner agencies to trace, restrain and recover corrupt funds, particularly money stolen from government agencies.

Commissioner Manning said Papua New Guinea is determined to exit the Financial Action Task Force [FATF] Grey List, adding that there can be no tolerance for dirty money, weak compliance or deliberate blindness.

He said the Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary, the Bank of Papua New Guinea's Financial Analysis and Supervision Unit, and the Office of the Public Prosecutor are working together under ARROW using modern analytical systems to identify unexplained wealth, suspicious transactions and financial movements that do not match lawful income.

The Commissioner also placed financial institutions on notice, saying banks and reporting entities must know their customers, question suspicious transactions and immediately report any red flags to police.

He warned that where public funds move through financial institutions without proper scrutiny, negligence, wilful blindness or facilitation will be investigated and criminal charges will follow.

"We are squeezing the corrupt and those who enable them. The money trail is no longer a safe place to hide," Commissioner Manning said.

item.title: One of two Baisu escapees shot dead
item.description: Of the two prisoners still on the run after the recent Baisu Correctional Institution breakout, one has been shot dead during a dawn raid by a joint Police and Correctional Service operation, leaving only one escapee still at large.

item.title: PNG Power selling electricity at a loss: Duma
item.description: PNG Power is selling electricity for less than it costs to buy it, outgoing minister William Duma says, warning that the pricing system has left the state-owned company struggling financially.
